lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market precision turned manufacturers machining products of metal, plastic, or composite materials on a recurring job or order basis for external customers using automatic screw machines, rotary transfer machines, CNC lathes, or turning centers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive plants, non-recurring prototype-only shops without repeat programs, manufacturers of standardized fasteners classified elsewhere, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the normalized EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: OEM and industrial customers buy recurring precision components under drawings, material, tolerance, inspection, traceability, packaging, and delivery requirements through purchase orders, blanket orders, or supply agreements.

a.bridge: BLS reports the broader fabricated-metal occupation mix and national machinist outlook rather than wage-weighted tasks in eligible 332721 firms, while ILO reports global GenAI exposure by broad occupation. I apply higher exposure to quoting, manufacturability review, programming support, purchasing, scheduling, inspection-data review, quality documentation, and administration and lower exposure to setup, machine tending, maintenance, physical inspection, deburring, washing, and material handling. The broad data may overstate welding and assembly but understate CNC programming and metrology work specific to turned products.
a.rationale: Precision turning is already CNC-intensive, so machine cycles and existing automation are excluded. Remaining opportunity lies in estimating, routing, program creation and verification, schedule optimization, quality records, tool-life analysis, and customer communication; setup and physical production remain less directly exposed.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task study across eligible precision-turning plants, separating current automated cycles from manual estimating, programming, setup, production, inspection, and administration, would materially change the range.
caveats: BLS occupation counts cover NAICS 332 rather than this six-digit industry.
ILO exposure is technical potential rather than observed substitution.
Existing CNC, bar feeding, probing, and robotic handling are not part of current unautomated opportunity.

rho.rationale: Digital drawings, CNC programs, inspection records, and machine data make quoting, planning, quality, and maintenance workflows implementable within five years. Customer validation, cybersecurity, legacy controls, program-verification risk, scarce labeled failure data, and responsibility for tight tolerances constrain rollout.
rho.change_evidence: Plant pilots measuring quote time, programming and prove-out time, schedule adherence, tool life, scrap, inspection false calls, accepted recommendations, and integration cost would materially change implementation.
caveats: No source measures five-year AI implementation in eligible precision-turning firms.
Modern connected turning centers and older cam-driven screw-machine fleets have very different readiness.

e.rationale: Census explicitly defines the industry as machining precision products on a job or order basis, and its manufacturing form separately identifies contract precision turning performed with customer-owned materials. The default outsourced-service model therefore fits most independent firms, subject to repeat-work, size, captive-status, and transferability exclusions.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm review of the 1,201 dataset firms using recurring job-order revenue, customer ownership of designs or materials, repeat-program share, captive status, prototype mix, and normalized EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
caveats: The provided firm count is margin-bridged and may not align precisely with the stated EBITDA band.
Job-order manufacturing can still be project-like if customers do not place repeat production orders.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 51% of responding owners of U.S. employer businesses across industries were age 55 or older, not qualifying control transfers in precision turning. I use this only as succession pressure and reduce it for the five-year window, internal succession, non-sale exits, multi-owner firms, equipment debt, customer approvals, concentration, and key-person dependence. The cross-industry proxy may overstate sale probability but may understate succession pressure in founder-led machining businesses.
s5.rationale: The large estimated target population and prevalence of independent job shops create plausible seller supply. A completed transfer still requires durable repeat programs, capable managers and programmers, maintained equipment, quality certifications, and reduced owner dependence.
s5.change_evidence: Closed machining-sector deal records, UCC and ownership changes, and interviews with precision-manufacturing brokers, owners, lenders, and quality registrars over the prior five years would materially change the probability.
caveats: Owner-age data are cross-industry, based on 2018, and count responding owners rather than firms.
Retirement need does not establish sale intent, business quality, or transaction completion.

q.rationale: Qualification, process capability, tooling knowledge, traceability, delivery performance, and switching cost can preserve benefits within repeat programs. Annual cost-down requests, competitive RFQs, should-cost analysis, metal surcharges, and concentrated OEM procurement pass visible productivity gains to customers.
q.change_evidence: A contract and RFQ sample showing term, rebid cadence, cost-down clauses, open-book provisions, material pass-through, tooling ownership, productivity sharing, and historical concessions would materially change retention.
caveats: No public contract dataset measures AI benefit pass-through for eligible firms.
Retention differs between highly qualified critical parts and readily portable commodity turned components.

d5.bridge: BLS real sectoral output and Federal Reserve industrial production measure the broader NAICS 3327 group, not five-year constant-price demand for eligible precision-turned job work. Real output fell from 102.578 in 2022 to 84.601 in 2025, while industrial production fell from 103.5785 in 2023 to 94.3232 in 2025. I translate this cyclical weakness into near-flat base demand with reshoring and recovery upside. The broader group may overstate weakness or strength depending on machine shops and fasteners and does not isolate qualified turned components.
d5.rationale: Precision turned parts remain inputs to vehicles, aerospace, medical devices, defense, fluid systems, electronics, and industrial machinery. Demand is diversified but cyclical and exposed to imports, customer inventory, product redesign, and program concentration; qualification and reshoring can support specialized suppliers.
d5.change_evidence: Customer build forecasts, bookings, backlog, current import penetration, awarded program life, and product-level shipment histories across eligible firms would materially change the ratio.
caveats: The output series combines machine shops, turned products, and fasteners and includes firms outside the lens.
Historical output is not a direct five-year forecast.
Aerospace, automotive, medical, and general-industrial programs have different cycles and qualification barriers.

o.rationale: Remaining demand requires physical turning, setup, tool management, deburring, cleaning, inspection, traceability, and accountable conformance. Software can improve programs and coordination but cannot deliver the qualified part; displacement is mainly customer insourcing, supplier consolidation, imports, additive manufacturing, or product redesign.
o.change_evidence: Customer make-versus-buy plans, supplier-consolidation records, additive-manufacturing qualifications, autonomous-cell evidence, and redesign programs eliminating turned components would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Operator requirement can persist while production shifts to larger or offshore suppliers.
Demand loss and imports are reflected mainly in d5.

narrative.executive_view: Precision turned manufacturing closely matches the recurring outsourced-service lens because Census defines it as job- or order-based production. The large estimated firm population and digital production environment create real workflow opportunity, although CNC already automates much of the direct cycle and broad industry output has weakened.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can improve RFQ parsing, estimating, manufacturability review, routing, CNC programming support, scheduling, tool-life analysis, inspection review, quality documentation, and customer communication. Setup, machine tending, maintenance, deburring, washing, and physical metrology remain operator- and equipment-dependent.
narrative.value_capture: Qualified repeat programs, process capability, tooling knowledge, and delivery performance create switching cost. OEM cost-down clauses, competitive RFQs, metal indices, and should-cost models can transfer visible savings, so value is more durable when it improves capacity, quality, and lead time.
narrative.firm_availability: Most independent precision turners fit the service model, and founder succession can generate seller supply. Transferability depends on repeat-program diversification, programmers and setup talent, quality systems, equipment condition, customer approvals, and reduced reliance on the owner.
narrative.demand_durability: Turned components remain necessary across diverse physical products, with specialized qualification supporting retention. Current broad output is weak, and demand remains exposed to program cycles, imports, inventory corrections, additive manufacturing, and redesign.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The case weakens if a target has mostly one-off prototypes, one dominant customer, obsolete or disconnected machines, undocumented tribal programming knowledge, heavy deferred capital needs, or contracts that force annual savings through. Quality escapes and cyber compromise of customer drawings are material risks.
narrative.principal_driver: Job-order manufacturing, repeat qualified programs, and digital CNC and inspection data create a strong base for AI-assisted operating workflows.
narrative.principal_weakness: Direct production is already automated, while broad output weakness and OEM cost-down pressure limit incremental labor and commercial upside.

sources.title: Precision Turned Product Manufacturing Profile
sources.supports: Census defines 332721 as machining precision products of all materials on a job or order basis, generally in large volumes using automatic screw machines, rotary transfer machines, CNC lathes, or turning centers.

sources.title: Fabricated Metal Product Manufacturing: NAICS 332
sources.supports: BLS reports 2025 fabricated-metal employment of 56,060 press operators, 67,090 production supervisors, 101,640 machinists, 125,250 team assemblers, and 112,520 welders.

sources.title: Generative AI and Jobs: Policies to Manage the Transition
sources.supports: ILO reports 24% of clerical tasks at high automation exposure and 58% at medium exposure, while other occupational groups have only 1% to 4% highly exposed and no more than 25% medium-exposed tasks.

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages
sources.supports: Census reports that 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older, based on the 2019 Annual Business Survey for data year 2018.

sources.title: Real Sectoral Output: Machine Shops, Turned Product, and Fasteners
sources.supports: BLS real sectoral output for NAICS 3327 declined from an index of 102.578 in 2022 to 84.601 in 2025, with 2017 equal to 100.

sources.title: Machinists and Tool and Die Makers
sources.supports: BLS projects machinist and tool-and-die employment to decline 2% from 2024 to 2034, describes use of CAD/CAM, CNC tools, and computerized measuring machines, and expects 34,200 openings annually from replacement needs.

sources.title: 2022 Census Manufacturing Product Classification Form for 33271
sources.supports: Census separately identifies automotive and other precision turned products made on CNC equipment or screw machines and contract precision turning performed with materials owned by customers.
